basmala: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
intro: Al-Muʾminūn — “The Believers” — opens on the great cry of victory: “The believers have prospered!” (qad aflaḥa-l-muʾminūn). Qushayrī makes of this falāḥ the triumph of the one who seizes what he sought: true success is not outward, it is the inscribing of the Real in the inmost being. The sūra then draws the portrait of the happy believer: humble in his prayer — and khushūʿ, says Qushayrī, is the bowing of the secret upon the carpet of intimate discourse, the effacement before the bursting-in of theophanies — turned from idle talk, giving the purifying alms, guarding his chastity, faithful to his trusts and covenants, watchful over his prayers: these are the inheritors of Firdaws. Then the account descends into the making of man — from the extract of clay to the drop, the clot, the lump, the bones clothed with flesh, up to “another creation”: “Blessed be God, the best of creators!” Come then the signs of heaven and earth, the measured water, the gardens; then Noah, the successive generations, Moses and Aaron, the son of Mary; the unity of the messengers and the unity of the community; the obstinate denial of resurrection and the great question: “Did you think We created you in vain, and that you would not be returned to Us?” The sūra is sealed on the sovereignty of the King, the Truth, Lord of the noble Throne, and on the final prayer: “My Lord, forgive and have mercy!”

versets.ar: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ
versets.tr: qad aflaḥa-l-muʾminūna
versets.trad: The believers have prospered!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: he who has believed in God has attained his goal and triumphed in his quest. Falāḥ is to win what one seeks and to seize what one aims at. Faith, he says, is the inscribing of the Real in the inmost being, the penetration of assent to the very core of the heart, the rooting of realization in the depths of the soul. The sūra thus opens on the believer's victory — not an outward success, but the seizing of meaning by the one in whom certitude has made its home.

mots.racine: ف ل ح
أ م ن
mots.def: muʾmin: one who has faith, who gives trust.

versets.ar: الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي صَلَاتِهِمْ خَاشِعُونَ
versets.tr: alladhīna hum fī ṣalātihim khāshiʿūna
versets.trad: those who are humble in their prayers,
versets.commentaire: Khushūʿ, for Qushayrī, is the bowing of the secret upon the carpet of intimate discourse, through the fullness of reverence, the melting under the sway of unveiling, the effacement before the bursting-in of theophanies. And he adds: he tastes the fruits of nearness and the fullness of intimacy who stands upon the carpet of confidence with reverence and keeps the courtesies of the Presence; but intimacy with the Beloved is consummated only at the disappearance of the intrusive watcher — and the most tenacious of intruders, the one who most spoils the hour of nearness, is the self: there is no rest for the one at prayer so long as his self is present to him.

mots.racine: ص ل و
خ ش ع
mots.def: khushūʿ: humility of heart, reverent stillness.

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنِ اللَّغْوِ مُعْرِضُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna hum ʿani-l-laghwi muʿriḍūna
versets.trad: those who turn away from idle talk,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives a graded definition of the vain: what turns one from God is distraction; what is not for God is filler; what is neither heard from God nor grasped in God's company, that is the vain (laghw). And again: of the speech of creatures, all that is not praise and celebration of God, all that is laghw. To turn from the vain is therefore to orient ear and tongue toward the Real alone.

mots.racine: ل غ و
mots.def: laghw: empty speech, what is without import.

mots.racine: ع ر ض
mots.def: iʿrāḍ: to turn away, to disregard.

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِلزَّكَاةِ فَاعِلُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna hum li-z-zakāti fāʿilūna
versets.trad: those who give the purifying alms,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī plays on the double sense of zakāt — alms and growth: zakāt is increase; and the sign of one who works for growth is that he decreases in his own eyes, lessening himself before what he contemplates. For, he adds, the servant attains the perfection of servanthood only by his melting away from his own self-witnessing. The alms that make the soul grow are those that strip it of its pretension.

mots.racine: ز ك و
mots.def: zakāt: legal alms; also purification, growth.

mots.racine: ف ع ل
mots.def: fāʿil: one who acts, who does.

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حَافِظُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna hum li-furūjihim ḥāfiẓūna
versets.trad: those who guard their chastity, save
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they guard their chastity for the sake of a progeny that will fulfil the rights of God; and this holds when their intent is continence and the preserving of self far from the transgressions of sin. Guarding chastity is not mere prohibition but orientation: making the purity of the body the service of an upright end.

mots.racine: ف ر ج
mots.def: farj: the intimate part, what must be guarded.

mots.racine: ح ف ظ

versets.ar: إِلَّا عَلَىٰ أَزْوَاجِهِمْ أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَانُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ
versets.tr: illā ʿalā azwājihim aw mā malakat aymānuhum fa-innahum ghayru malūmīna
versets.trad: with their wives and those their right hands possess; for then they are not to be blamed,
versets.commentaire: The exception opens the space of the licit: union within the bounds God has set draws no reproach. In Qushayrī's spirit, what keeps within the measure willed by the Real escapes blame; blame falls only upon the one who oversteps the limit and seeks the gratifying of his bent beyond the right. Here the servant remains within courtesy and stays unharmed.

mots.racine: ز و ج
ل و م

versets.ar: فَمَنِ ابْتَغَىٰ وَرَاءَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْعَادُونَ
versets.tr: fa-mani-btaghā warāʾa dhālika fa-ulāʾika humu-l-ʿādūna
versets.trad: but whoever seeks beyond that — those are the transgressors;
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: whoever passes beyond the concern to prefer the rights, and inclines toward the side of claiming his shares, has transgressed the place of the greatest and departed from their way. The transgressor (ʿādī) is not merely one who breaks a rule, but one who, deserting the right, runs after his own enjoyment.

mots.racine: ب غ ي
ع د و
mots.def: ʿādī: one who oversteps the limit.

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَمَانَاتِهِمْ وَعَهْدِهِمْ رَاعُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna hum li-amānātihim wa-ʿahdihim rāʿūna
versets.trad: those who keep their trusts and their covenants,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes the trusts a ladder: trusts are diverse, and each one bears another: for some the outward obligations, for others the subtleties deposited in the secret; for some their dealings, for others their spiritual descents, for yet others their unions. Likewise the covenants: one has pledged to worship none but Him; another has pledged to witness none but Him in the two worlds.

mots.racine: أ م ن
mots.def: amāna: entrusted deposit, what binds fidelity.

mots.racine: ع ه د / ر ع ي
mots.def: ʿahd: covenant; raʿā: to keep, to observe.

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَوَاتِهِمْ يُحَافِظُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna hum ʿalā ṣalawātihim yuḥāfiẓūna
versets.trad: those who are watchful over their prayers,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī paints the watchfulness of the faithful one at prayer: the hours never surprise them unready; the caller never summons them but they are already at the door. They are in the first rank by their outward, and just as much in the first rank by their secret. Guarding prayer is not mere punctuality but the anticipated presence of the heart.

mots.racine: ص ل و
ح ف ظ

versets.ar: أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْوَارِثُونَ
versets.tr: ulāʾika humu-l-wārithūna
versets.trad: those are the inheritors:
versets.commentaire: Spiritual inheritance is settled, Qushayrī says, according to kinship: one inherits Firdaws by way of inheritance, through the kinship of faith at root, then through acts of obedience in surplus. As in bodily inheritance the shares differ, so too in works: to each according to his bond and his fidelity.

mots.racine: و ر ث

versets.ar: الَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ الْفِرْدَوْسَ هُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ
versets.tr: alladhīna yarithūna-l-firdawsa hum fīhā khālidūna
versets.trad: who will inherit Paradise, abiding therein forever.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: among them, some are in Firdaws by their soul, and in the subtle states by their heart; then they abide there eternally, soul and heart together, never leaving the enjoyment of their soul nor the states of their heart. Firdaws, the highest garden, is not merely a place but the permanence of the gift.

mots.racine: و ر ث / ف ر د س
mots.def: Firdaws: the highest of the gardens of Paradise.

mots.racine: خ ل د

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ مِن سُلَالَةٍ مِّن طِينٍ
versets.tr: wa-laqad khalaqnā-l-insāna min sulālatin min ṭīnin
versets.trad: We created man from an extract of clay,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī multiplies the senses: He made known to them their origin so they would not glory in their deeds; He traced their lineage so they would not stray from their measure. And again: He drew them from an extract taken from every region — hard earth, salt-flat, plain or rugged ground — and that is why their characters differ. But above all: He created them from clay, and yet this clay is the lode of gnosis, the pasture of love, the point where His solicitude is fastened: « He loves them and they love Him. »

mots.racine: خ ل ق / أ ن س
س ل ل / ط ي ن
mots.def: sulāla: quintessence drawn out; ṭīn: clay.

versets.ar: ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَاهُ نُطْفَةً فِي قَرَارٍ مَّكِينٍ
versets.tr: thumma jaʿalnāhu nuṭfatan fī qarārin makīnin
versets.trad: then We made him a drop in a secure lodging;
versets.commentaire: From clay to drop: the account descends into the detail of the human making. Qushayrī marvels at the unity within this drop: a drop whose parts resemble one another, a fluid whose elements echo one another — and from this indistinction God will draw the diversity of the limbs. The « secure lodging » (qarār makīn) is the crucible where, under measure, the mystery of becoming is worked.

mots.racine: ن ط ف
ق ر ر / م ك ن
mots.def: qarār: place of lodging; makīn: firm, established.

versets.ar: ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا النُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْمُضْغَةَ عِظَامًا فَكَسَوْنَا الْعِظَامَ لَحْمًا ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَاهُ خَلْقًا آخَرَ فَتَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخَالِقِينَ
versets.tr: thumma khalaqnā-n-nuṭfata ʿalaqatan fa-khalaqnā-l-ʿalaqata muḍghatan fa-khalaqnā-l-muḍghata ʿiẓāman fa-kasawnā-l-ʿiẓāma laḥman thumma anshaʾnāhu khalqan ākhara fa-tabāraka-llāhu aḥsanu-l-khāliqīna
versets.trad: then from the drop We created a clot, and from the clot a lump, and from the lump We created bones, and We clothed the bones with flesh; then We brought him forth as another creation. So blessed be God, the best of creators!
versets.commentaire: On « another creation » (khalqan ākhar), Qushayrī sees the noble states God brings to flower in the man come to maturity: to one falls the adornment of servanthood, to another deliverance from carnal bondage, to yet another realization through the attributes of the Absolute, by the effacement of all sensation of what he is. And on « the best of creators »: He created heavens, earths, Throne and Footstool, the Garden and the Fire — and followed none of these works with this praise; but after describing the creation of Adam, He uttered it, distinguishing man among all creatures. If once you had no tongue to thank Him nor mouth to praise Him, He Himself stood in for your praise, saying: « Blessed be God, the best of creators. »

mots.racine: ع ل ق
م ض غ
ك س و / ع ظ م / ل ح م
mots.sens: We clothed the bones with flesh
mots.def: kasā: to clothe; ʿiẓām: bones; laḥm: flesh.

mots.racine: ح س ن / خ ل ق
mots.def: aḥsan: the finest, the best; khāliq: creator.

versets.ar: ثُمَّ إِنَّكُم بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ لَمَيِّتُونَ
versets.tr: thumma innakum baʿda dhālika la-mayyitūna
versets.trad: Then, after that, you shall surely die,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī hears this verse as a death-notice addressed to the soul: He announced to you your own death — « after that, you shall surely die » — and all that is coming is near. He sees in it too a blow to the pride of the heedless: He shattered the conceit of their negligence by telling them they will be like inert stone, far from all power and force, counted among the weightless dead.

mots.racine: ب ع د
م و ت

versets.ar: ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ تُبْعَثُونَ
versets.tr: thumma innakum yawma-l-qiyāmati tubʿathūna
versets.trad: then on the Day of Resurrection you shall be raised.
versets.commentaire: Death gives way to awakening. Qushayrī: then come the reckoning and the chastisement, the questioning, and the accepted is set apart from the rejected, the brought-near from the abandoned. And he says of the Day: it is a Day the world so dreads that, were the Resurrection asked, “What do you fear?”, it would answer, “The Resurrection.” You will see people there drunk, bewildered, unaware of their plight, until for each his case is made plain, his good and his evil.

mots.racine: ي و م / ق و م
ب ع ث

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعَ طَرَائِقَ وَمَا كُنَّا عَنِ الْخَلْقِ غَافِلِينَ
versets.tr: wa-laqad khalaqnā fawqakum sabʿa ṭarāʾiqa wa-mā kunnā ʿani-l-khalqi ghāfilīna
versets.trad: We created above you seven paths, and We were not heedless of the creation.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī transposes the « seven paths » into veils set over the heart: above us are outward veils — the heavens that part us from the lofty abodes — and inward veils upon the hearts: covetousness, passion, the engrossing wills, the heaped-up heedlessnesses. The aspirant overshadowed by the clouds of slackening, the renouncer crossed by the vein of desire, the gnostic upon whom falls a pause in the ascent of the secret — all undergo these « paths ». And in all this, the Real is never heedless of His creation, never does He abandon His servants.

mots.racine: س ب ع / ط ر ق
خ ل ق / غ ف ل

versets.ar: وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً بِقَدَرٍ فَأَسْكَنَّاهُ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰ ذَهَابٍ بِهِ لَقَادِرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-anzalnā mina-s-samāʾi māʾan bi-qadarin fa-askannāhu fī-l-arḍi wa-innā ʿalā dhahābin bihi la-qādirūna
versets.trad: And We sent down from the sky water in measure, and We lodged it in the earth, though We are able to make it vanish.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes the rain the symbol of the grace that waters hearts: likewise We send down from the sky the water of mercy, which revives the hearts; and they differ in their drinking: to one the provision is enlarged, to another stinted; there is a time of downpour and a time of withholding. And he distinguishes three waters: the water of mercy that washes away the filth of sinners; the water that gives the hearts to drink and dispels the thirst of their bewilderment; and the water of the draught of love, which wins certain hearts to the courtyards of nearness — when they drink it, they are enraptured, and enraptured, they care no more for what they give, were it their very soul.

mots.racine: م و ه / ق د ر
س ك ن / أ ر ض
mots.sens: We lodged it in the earth

versets.ar: فَأَنشَأْنَا لَكُم بِهِ جَنَّاتٍ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَأَعْنَابٍ لَّكُمْ فِيهَا فَوَاكِهُ كَثِيرَةٌ وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ
versets.tr: fa-anshaʾnā lakum bihi jannātin min nakhīlin wa-aʿnābin lakum fīhā fawākihu kathīratun wa-minhā taʾkulūna
versets.trad: By it We brought forth for you gardens of palms and vines, in which are abundant fruits whereof you eat,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reads in these orchards a parable of the watered heart: as the water of the sky revives the groves and gardens, brings to bloom flowers and lights there, makes the trees fruit and the rivers flow, so the hearts are watered with the water of gnosis: they bud and fruit after blossoming, and yield their harvest — sweetness of living, fullness of expansion, then abundance of reverence, then the breath of intimacy, fruits of theophany and returns of nearness — things too short for expressions to describe, that allusions despair of encompassing.

mots.racine: ج ن ن / ن خ ل / ع ن ب
ف ك ه / ك ث ر

versets.ar: وَشَجَرَةً تَخْرُجُ مِن طُورِ سَيْنَاءَ تَنبُتُ بِالدُّهْنِ وَصِبْغٍ لِّلْآكِلِينَ
versets.tr: wa-shajaratan takhruju min ṭūri saynāʾa tanbutu bi-d-duhni wa-ṣibghin li-l-ākilīna
versets.trad: and a tree (the olive) that springs from Mount Sinai, yielding oil and a relish for those who eat.
versets.commentaire: The olive risen from Sinai — the mountain of Moses's confidence — gives oil and seasoning. In Qushayrī's spirit, these signs of the earth are so many invitations to recognize the hand of the Provider: the beneficial tree, rooted in a place of revelation, recalls that the body's sustenance bears the trace of a holy provenance. The light of the oil, elsewhere likened to the light of God, makes of this tree a sign for one who can read.

mots.racine: ش ج ر / ط و ر
mots.sens: a tree springing from Mount Sinai
mots.def: shajara: tree; Ṭūr Saynāʾ: Mount Sinai.

mots.racine: ن ب ت / د ه ن

versets.ar: وَإِنَّ لَكُمْ فِي الْأَنْعَامِ لَعِبْرَةً نُّسْقِيكُم مِّمَّا فِي بُطُونِهَا وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَافِعُ كَثِيرَةٌ وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ
versets.tr: wa-inna lakum fī-l-anʿāmi la-ʿibratan nusqīkum mimmā fī buṭūnihā wa-lakum fīhā manāfiʿu kathīratun wa-minhā taʾkulūna
versets.trad: And surely in the cattle there is a lesson for you: We give you to drink of what is in their bellies, and in them are many benefits for you, and of them you eat;
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī draws from the milk an ishāra on the purity that wells up from the impure: the troubles that arise do not count: pure, palatable milk comes from the bellies of the beasts, from amid what their entrails enclose, and yet it stays limpid, untouched by their defilement. Thus limpidity most often springs from the very source of trouble — for Reality itself clings to neither truth nor falsehood. He who rises to the secret of Unity recognizes that every phenomenon comes from the Decree: the burden of discernment then falls from him, the secrets grow pure, and time no longer betrays its master.

mots.racine: ن ع م / ع ب ر
س ق ي / ب ط ن
mots.sens: We give you drink from their bellies
mots.def: saqā: to give drink; baṭn: belly.

versets.ar: وَعَلَيْهَا وَعَلَى الْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ
versets.tr: wa-ʿalayhā wa-ʿalā-l-fulki tuḥmalūna
versets.trad: and upon them and upon the ship you are carried.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes the ship the image of salvation: He keeps them in the vessel upon the seas of water, and He keeps them in the vessel of safeguarding and preservation upon the seas of Power; for the seas of Power surge their waves, and people are drowned in them, save whom the Real preserves in the vessel of His solicitude. And he cites this verse: “People are a deep sea — to keep far from them is a ship; I have counseled you, so look to your poor soul.”

mots.racine: ف ل ك
ح م ل

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِ فَقَالَ يَا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَٰهٍ غَيْرُهُ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ
versets.tr: wa-laqad arsalnā nūḥan ilā qawmihi fa-qāla yā qawmi-ʿbudū-llāha mā lakum min ilāhin ghayruhu a-fa-lā tattaqūna
versets.trad: We sent Noah to his people, and he said: “O my people! Worship God! You have no god but Him! Will you not be God-fearing?”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī explains why the account of Noah returns: because of the greatness of its signs — the length of his stay among his people, the harshness of the trial endured, the fullness of his patience throughout an immense life, then the annihilation of all who persisted in denial. He relates the tradition: his name was Yashkur, but from his much weeping God revealed to him: “O Noah, how long will you wail (tanūḥ)?” — and He named him Nūḥ. Five centuries of calling: the more he summoned them, the more they fled his answer.

mots.racine: ر س ل
ع ب د

versets.ar: فَقَالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِن قَوْمِهِ مَا هَٰذَا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ يُرِيدُ أَن يَتَفَضَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ لَأَنزَلَ مَلَائِكَةً مَّا سَمِعْنَا بِهَٰذَا فِي آبَائِنَا الْأَوَّلِينَ
versets.tr: fa-qāla-l-malaʾu-lladhīna kafarū min qawmihi mā hādhā illā basharun mithlukum yurīdu an yatafaḍḍala ʿalaykum wa-law shāʾa-llāhu la-anzala malāʾikatan mā samiʿnā bi-hādhā fī ābāʾinā-l-awwalīna
versets.trad: The chiefs of his people who disbelieved said: “This is but a mortal like you; he wishes to set himself above you. Had God willed, He would have sent down angels. We never heard of this among our forefathers of old.
themes: the argument of the prophet's humanity
versets.commentaire: The chiefs' objection is always the same: a prophet cannot be a man “like us.” Qushayrī sees in it the blindness of one who measures the possible only by the customary: they hold election improbable because it is foreign to them. They invoke their “forefathers of old” as an unsurpassable norm, prisoners of imitation, closed to the unheard-of grace.

mots.racine: م ل أ / ك ف ر
mots.def: malaʾ: assembly of leaders; kafara: to deny.

mots.racine: ب ش ر / م ث ل

versets.ar: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا رَجُلٌ بِهِ جِنَّةٌ فَتَرَبَّصُوا بِهِ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ
versets.tr: in huwa illā rajulun bihi jinnatun fa-tarabbaṣū bihi ḥattā ḥīnin
versets.trad: He is but a man possessed; so wait with him for a while!”
versets.commentaire: The charge of possession (jinna) recurs as the reflex of deniers before what exceeds them. Qushayrī recalls elsewhere that such men, unable to conceive of election, attribute to madness what they do not understand. “Wait a while”: they wager on time to wear down the messenger, but it is their own term that draws near.

mots.racine: ج ن ن
ر ب ص

versets.ar: قَالَ رَبِّ انصُرْنِي بِمَا كَذَّبُونِ
versets.tr: qāla rabbi-nṣurnī bimā kadhdhabūni
versets.trad: He said: “My Lord! Help me against their denial!”
versets.commentaire: Faced with the denial, Noah does not defend himself: he turns to his Lord. In Qushayrī's spirit, this is the reflex of the true servant — to commit his cause to the Real rather than to take justice himself. The prayer “help me” is not a request for vengeance but a surrender: that God take charge of what man cannot bear.

mots.racine: ن ص ر
ك ذ ب

versets.ar: فَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْهِ أَنِ اصْنَعِ الْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا وَوَحْيِنَا فَإِذَا جَاءَ أَمْرُنَا وَفَارَ التَّنُّورُ فَاسْلُكْ فِيهَا مِن كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَن سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ الْقَوْلُ مِنْهُمْ وَلَا تُخَاطِبْنِي فِي الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا إِنَّهُم مُّغْرَقُونَ
versets.tr: fa-awḥaynā ilayhi ani-ṣnaʿi-l-fulka bi-aʿyuninā wa-waḥyinā fa-idhā jāʾa amrunā wa-fāra-t-tannūru fa-sluk fīhā min kullin zawjayni-thnayni wa-ahlaka illā man sabaqa ʿalayhi-l-qawlu minhum wa-lā tukhāṭibnī fī-lladhīna ẓalamū innahum mughraqūna
versets.trad: So We revealed to him: “Build the ship under Our eyes and Our inspiration. When Our command comes and the oven boils over, take into it of every kind a pair, and your family — except those against whom the word has already gone forth. And do not address Me concerning those who have done wrong: they shall be drowned.
versets.commentaire: “Under Our eyes” (bi-aʿyuninā): the building of the ark is done under God's gaze and inspiration. Qushayrī relates the famous episode: when Noah had built the ark and embarked his people, Iblīs came and asked to be taken aboard. Noah refused: “O wretch, you hope I would carry you, the head of the deniers?” Iblīs answered: “Do you not know, O Noah, that God has granted me respite until the Day of Resurrection, and that none is saved today but in this vessel?” God then revealed to Noah to take him aboard. Yet Noah's own son found no place there: here appears the very eye of Unity — God's decree has no assignable cause; He grants to whom He wills and rejects whom He wills.

mots.racine: ص ن ع / ف ل ك / ع ي ن
mots.sens: build the ark under Our eyes
mots.def: ṣanaʿa: to build; aʿyun: eyes, gaze.

mots.racine: ف و ر / ت ن ر
ز و ج

versets.ar: فَإِذَا اسْتَوَيْتَ أَنتَ وَمَن مَّعَكَ عَلَى الْفُلْكِ فَقُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي نَجَّانَا مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ
versets.tr: fa-idhā-stawayta anta wa-man maʿaka ʿalā-l-fulki fa-quli-l-ḥamdu lillāhi-lladhī najjānā mina-l-qawmi-ẓ-ẓālimīna
versets.trad: When you and those with you are settled in the ship, say: “Praise be to God who has saved us from the wrongdoing people!”
versets.commentaire: Salvation at once calls for praise. In Qushayrī's pedagogy, the rescued servant takes nothing for himself: he refers the deliverance to its sole Author. To say “praise be to God who has saved us” is to inscribe gratitude at the very heart of the trial passed through, to make of every deliverance an occasion to turn back to the Savior.

mots.racine: س و ي
mots.def: istawā: to be established, to sit firm.

mots.racine: ح م د / ن ج و
mots.sens: praise be to God who saved us

versets.ar: وَقُل رَّبِّ أَنزِلْنِي مُنزَلًا مُّبَارَكًا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ الْمُنزِلِينَ
versets.tr: wa-qul rabbi anzilnī munzalan mubārakan wa-anta khayru-l-munzilīna
versets.trad: And say: “My Lord! Bring me to a blessed landing; You are the best of those who bring to shore!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī raises the « blessed landing » to extinction: the blessed landing is that which is by God and for God, in the contemplation of God without any distraction from Him. It is absorption by the contemplation of the Attribute apart from you, then engulfment under the sway of nearness, then annihilation under the blaze of theophanies, until neither trace nor vestige remains. When this is accomplished and endures, it is a landing upon the courtyards of Reality — blessed because you are there without yourself, in your entirety, with no remnant or trace of you.

mots.racine: ن ز ل / ب ر ك
mots.sens: bring me to a blessed landing
mots.def: munzal: place of landing; mubārak: blessed.

mots.racine: خ ي ر / ن ز ل
mots.sens: the best of those who bring down
mots.def: khayr: the best; munzil: who brings down.

versets.ar: إِنَّ فِي ذَٰلِكَ لَآيَاتٍ وَإِن كُنَّا لَمُبْتَلِينَ
versets.tr: inna fī dhālika la-āyātin wa-in kunnā la-mubtalīna
versets.trad: Surely in that are signs, and indeed We are ever testing.
versets.commentaire: The story of Noah is a cluster of signs (āyāt) for one who can read. “We are ever testing”: the trial (ibtilāʾ) is no blind chastisement but a revealing of hearts. In Qushayrī's perspective, God tests in order to set apart the faithful from the denier, and each account of the ancients becomes a trial for the generations that hear it.

mots.racine: أ ي ي
ب ل و

versets.ar: ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَا مِن بَعْدِهِمْ قَرْنًا آخَرِينَ
versets.tr: thumma anshaʾnā min baʿdihim qarnan ākharīna
versets.trad: Then after them We raised up another generation,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī describes the stubborn cycle of peoples: the generations succeeded one another upon a single road of denial; the length of the respite deceived them, and the ease of life and the comfort of the rest in which He had established them. They measured themselves only against themselves, raised their eyes to none higher, and said: “Shall we believe in one who goes about in the markets and profits from the comforts as we do?” The pride of ease closes the heart to the call.

mots.racine: ن ش أ
mots.def: anshaʾa: to bring into being, to raise.

mots.racine: ق ر ن / أ خ ر

versets.ar: فَأَرْسَلْنَا فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَٰهٍ غَيْرُهُ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ
versets.tr: fa-arsalnā fīhim rasūlan minhum ani-ʿbudū-llāha mā lakum min ilāhin ghayruhu a-fa-lā tattaqūna
versets.trad: and We sent among them a messenger from themselves: “Worship God! You have no god but Him! Will you not be God-fearing?”
versets.commentaire: The same call rings out, generation after generation: to worship the one God. Qushayrī stresses the constancy of the message across the diversity of messengers — it is always the same invitation to reverent fear (taqwā). That the messenger is “from among them” is a grace: God speaks to each people through a voice it can recognize, but pride turns it into a grievance.

mots.racine: ر س ل
و ق ي

versets.ar: وَقَالَ الْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِهِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِلِقَاءِ الْآخِرَةِ وَأَتْرَفْنَاهُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا مَا هَٰذَا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ يَأْكُلُ مِمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ مِنْهُ وَيَشْرَبُ مِمَّا تَشْرَبُونَ
versets.tr: wa-qāla-l-malaʾu min qawmihi-lladhīna kafarū wa-kadhdhabū bi-liqāʾi-l-ākhirati wa-atrafnāhum fī-l-ḥayāti-d-dunyā mā hādhā illā basharun mithlukum yaʾkulu mimmā taʾkulūna minhu wa-yashrabu mimmā tashrabūna
versets.trad: And the chiefs of his people who disbelieved and denied the meeting of the Hereafter, and whom We had given ease in the life of this world, said: “This is but a mortal like you; he eats of what you eat and drinks of what you drink.
versets.commentaire: Three traits define these chiefs: denial, refusal of the “meeting” of the Hereafter, and the ease with which God had filled them. Qushayrī shows that it is precisely comfort that blinds: sated, they cannot conceive that a man “who eats and drinks as they do” could bear an election. Well-being becomes the very argument of disbelief.

mots.racine: ك ذ ب / ل ق ي / أ خ ر
mots.sens: they denied the meeting of the Hereafter
mots.def: liqāʾ: meeting; ākhira: the last life.

mots.racine: ت ر ف

versets.ar: وَلَئِنْ أَطَعْتُم بَشَرًا مِّثْلَكُمْ إِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّخَاسِرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-laʾin aṭaʿtum basharan mithlakum innakum idhan la-khāsirūna
versets.trad: And if you obey a mortal like yourselves, you will surely be losers.
versets.commentaire: The deniers turn the argument around: to obey “a mortal like you” would be ruin. Qushayrī shows the tragic irony: they call loss what would be their salvation, and take their own blindness for wisdom. The true khusrān — the loss — will be precisely theirs, for refusing the voice come to save them.

mots.racine: ط و ع / ب ش ر
mots.sens: you obey a human like you

mots.racine: خ س ر

versets.ar: أَيَعِدُكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ إِذَا مِتُّمْ وَكُنتُمْ تُرَابًا وَعِظَامًا أَنَّكُم مُّخْرَجُونَ
versets.tr: a-yaʿidukum annakum idhā mittum wa-kuntum turāban wa-ʿiẓāman annakum mukhrajūna
versets.trad: Does he promise you that, once dead and turned to dust and bones, you will be brought forth?
versets.commentaire: The central objection returns: how to be reborn after dust and bones? Qushayrī recalls that this denial of resurrection is the recurrent error of peoples; they failed to rise to the truth that the recommencing is as possible as the commencing, and entails no impossibility. To measure Power by the gauge of their impotence is their constant fault.

mots.racine: م و ت / ت ر ب
خ ر ج
mots.def: akhraja: to bring forth, to resurrect.

versets.ar: هَيْهَاتَ هَيْهَاتَ لِمَا تُوعَدُونَ
versets.tr: hayhāta hayhāta limā tūʿadūna
versets.trad: Far, far-fetched is what you are promised!
versets.commentaire: “Hayhāt!” — an exclamation of derision marking, in their eyes, sheer improbability. For Qushayrī, this repeated rejection betrays the depth of their heedlessness: what they judge impossible is in truth what awaits them. The sarcasm against the promise is the last rampart of a heart that refuses to look at its end.

mots.def: hayhāt: interjection of remoteness, « it is improbable ».

mots.racine: و ع د

versets.ar: إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا الدُّنْيَا نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيَا وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ
versets.tr: in hiya illā ḥayātunā-d-dunyā namūtu wa-naḥyā wa-mā naḥnu bi-mabʿūthīna
versets.trad: There is only our life in this world: we die and we live, and we shall not be raised!
versets.commentaire: The creed of the deniers: there is no life but this one below. Qushayrī sees in it the confinement to the visible, the inability to conceive of a beyond the sensible. To reduce existence to the cycle “we die, we live” with no raising-up is to lower man to the rank of the perishable and to strip him of the very meaning of his end.

mots.racine: ح ي ي / د ن و
ب ع ث

versets.ar: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا رَجُلٌ افْتَرَىٰ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِبًا وَمَا نَحْنُ لَهُ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: in huwa illā rajulun-iftarā ʿalā-llāhi kadhiban wa-mā naḥnu lahu bi-muʾminīna
versets.trad: He is but a man who has invented a lie against God, and we do not believe in him!”
versets.commentaire: Unable to refute the message, they accuse the messenger of imposture. Qushayrī shows the mechanism of refusal: unable to deny the coherence of the call, one attacks its source. “We do not believe in him”: the decision of disbelief precedes the examination, and forecloses all hearing.

mots.racine: ف ر ي / ك ذ ب
mots.sens: he forged a lie against God
mots.def: iftarā: to invent, to slander; kadhib: lie.

mots.racine: أ م ن

versets.ar: قَالَ رَبِّ انصُرْنِي بِمَا كَذَّبُونِ
versets.tr: qāla rabbi-nṣurnī bimā kadhdhabūni
versets.trad: He said: “My Lord! Help me against their denial!”
versets.commentaire: The same prayer as Noah's returns on the lips of the rejected messenger: to commit his cause to God. Qushayrī makes of this repetition a teaching: the way of the prophets, before denial, is not riposte but recourse. The true servant does not avenge himself; he entrusts to the Real the task of judging.

mots.racine: ن ص ر
ك ذ ب

versets.ar: قَالَ عَمَّا قَلِيلٍ لَّيُصْبِحُنَّ نَادِمِينَ
versets.tr: qāla ʿammā qalīlin la-yuṣbiḥunna nādimīna
versets.trad: He said: “In a little while they will surely be regretful.”
versets.commentaire: The divine answer is brief and cutting: regret is near. Qushayrī recalls elsewhere that the term of their seizing is short, and the chastisement, when they are seized, is terrible. The “little while” measures the distance between present arrogance and the collapse to come: what seems far is already at the door.

mots.racine: ق ل ل
ص ب ح / ن د م

versets.ar: فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّيْحَةُ بِالْحَقِّ فَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ غُثَاءً فَبُعْدًا لِّلْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ
versets.tr: fa-akhadhat-humu-ṣ-ṣayḥatu bi-l-ḥaqqi fa-jaʿalnāhum ghuthāʾan fa-buʿdan li-l-qawmi-ẓ-ẓālimīna
versets.trad: Then the Cry seized them in truth, and We made them as scum. Away with the wrongdoing people!
versets.commentaire: The “Cry” (ṣayḥa) — the sudden chastisement — reduces them to debris swept by the torrent (ghuthāʾ). Qushayrī recalls that God grants respite but does not neglect; and when He seizes, His grip is violent. “Away with the wrongdoers!” seals their banishment from all mercy: he who has held far from the call finds himself forever far off.

mots.racine: ص ي ح / ح ق ق
غ ث و

versets.ar: ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَا مِن بَعْدِهِمْ قُرُونًا آخَرِينَ
versets.tr: thumma anshaʾnā min baʿdihim qurūnan ākharīna
versets.trad: Then after them We brought forth other generations.
versets.commentaire: The cycle continues: after the engulfed peoples, others arise. Qushayrī sees in this succession the patience of God renewing the call across the ages, but also the warning: no generation escapes the testing. The history of men is a series of chances offered, and answers weighed.

mots.racine: ق ر ن / أ خ ر
ب ع د

versets.ar: مَا تَسْبِقُ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلَهَا وَمَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ
versets.tr: mā tasbiqu min ummatin ajalahā wa-mā yastaʾkhirūna
versets.trad: No community can outstrip its term, nor put it back.
versets.commentaire: Each community has its term (ajal) which nothing outruns or delays. In Qushayrī's doctrine, this affirms the absolute sovereignty of the Decree: when a verdict issues from the Unseen, none can repel it. The time of peoples is not given over to chance but sealed with a measure that no human power can bend.

mots.racine: س ب ق / أ م م / أ ج ل
أ خ ر

versets.ar: ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلَنَا تَتْرَىٰ كُلَّ مَا جَاءَ أُمَّةً رَّسُولُهَا كَذَّبُوهُ فَأَتْبَعْنَا بَعْضَهُم بَعْضًا وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَحَادِيثَ فَبُعْدًا لِّقَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ
versets.tr: thumma arsalnā rusulanā tatrā kulla mā jāʾa ummatan rasūluhā kadhdhabūhu fa-atbaʿnā baʿḍahum baʿḍan wa-jaʿalnāhum aḥādītha fa-buʿdan li-qawmin lā yuʾminūna
versets.trad: Then We sent Our messengers one after another. Whenever its messenger came to a community, they denied him; so We made them follow one another, and made them mere tales. Away with a people who do not believe!
versets.commentaire: The messengers follow “one after another” (tatrā), and each time the same denial, the same annihilation. Qushayrī sees in this litany the deep unity of the prophetic mission and the constancy of human refusal. Become “mere tales” (aḥādīth), the vanished peoples serve as warning: history itself preaches the truth the living refuse.

mots.racine: ر س ل / و ت ر
mots.def: tatrā: in succession, one after another.

mots.racine: ح د ث

versets.ar: ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَأَخَاهُ هَارُونَ بِآيَاتِنَا وَسُلْطَانٍ مُّبِينٍ
versets.tr: thumma arsalnā mūsā wa-akhāhu hārūna bi-āyātinā wa-sulṭānin mubīnin
versets.trad: Then We sent Moses and his brother Aaron with Our signs and a clear authority,
versets.commentaire: Moses and Aaron are sent “with Our signs and a clear authority.” Qushayrī notes that God distinguished each prophet by his dazzling signs and his manifest miracles. The joint sending of the two brothers also speaks of solidarity in the mission: the prophetic burden is shared, and the “clear authority” (sulṭān mubīn) is the luminous proof that nothing human could counterfeit.

mots.racine: أ خ و
س ل ط / ب ي ن

versets.ar: إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَئِهِ فَاسْتَكْبَرُوا وَكَانُوا قَوْمًا عَالِينَ
versets.tr: ilā firʿawna wa-malaʾihi fa-stakbarū wa-kānū qawman ʿālīna
versets.trad: to Pharaoh and his chiefs; but they grew arrogant, and were a haughty people.
versets.commentaire: Pharaoh and his chiefs “grew arrogant” (istakbarū). Qushayrī makes arrogance the root of refusal: the heart that raises itself above the truth cannot bow before it. “A haughty people” (ʿālīn): pride is the thickest veil, for it lays claim to a height belonging to God alone, and makes of the creature an imagined rival of its Lord.

mots.racine: م ل أ
mots.def: Firʿawn: Pharaoh; malaʾ: assembly of leaders.

mots.racine: ك ب ر

versets.ar: فَقَالُوا أَنُؤْمِنُ لِبَشَرَيْنِ مِثْلِنَا وَقَوْمُهُمَا لَنَا عَابِدُونَ
versets.tr: fa-qālū a-nuʾminu li-basharayni mithlinā wa-qawmuhumā lanā ʿābidūna
versets.trad: They said: “Shall we believe in two mortals like ourselves, whose people are servants to us?”
versets.commentaire: The same argument as the chiefs of Noah: to believe in “two mortals like us” whose people “serve us” seems demeaning. Qushayrī shows that the pride of domination redoubles the blindness: not only is the prophet a man, he belongs to the despised people. Social arrogance thus becomes the rampart of disbelief.

mots.racine: أ م ن / ب ش ر
mots.sens: shall we believe in two humans like us?

mots.racine: ع ب د

versets.ar: فَكَذَّبُوهُمَا فَكَانُوا مِنَ الْمُهْلَكِينَ
versets.tr: fa-kadhdhabūhumā fa-kānū mina-l-muhlakīna
versets.trad: So they denied them both, and were among those destroyed.
versets.commentaire: Denial calls forth annihilation: Pharaoh and his people were among the “destroyed” (muhlakīn). Qushayrī inscribes this end within the constant law of the accounts: he who denies the messengers of God seals his own ruin. The drowning of Pharaoh becomes the archetype of the fate reserved for the pride that refuses to bend.

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
ه ل ك

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَى الْكِتَابَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ
versets.tr: wa-laqad ātaynā mūsā-l-kitāba laʿallahum yahtadūna
versets.trad: And We gave Moses the Book, that they might be guided.
versets.commentaire: The gift of the Book to Moses has guidance (hidāya) for its end. Qushayrī recalls that Revelation is a light offered: it does not compel but illumines. “That they might be guided” voices God's merciful intent — the Book is held out like a hand, and it is for the heart to seize it or turn away.

mots.racine: أ ت ي / ك ت ب
mots.def: ātā: to give; kitāb: Book, Scripture.

mots.racine: ه د ي

versets.ar: وَجَعَلْنَا ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَأُمَّهُ آيَةً وَآوَيْنَاهُمَا إِلَىٰ رَبْوَةٍ ذَاتِ قَرَارٍ وَمَعِينٍ
versets.tr: wa-jaʿalnā-bna maryama wa-ummahu āyatan wa-āwaynāhumā ilā rabwatin dhāti qarārin wa-maʿīnin
versets.trad: And We made the son of Mary and his mother a sign, and gave them refuge upon a peaceful height with a flowing spring.
versets.commentaire: The son of Mary and his mother are themselves “a sign” (āya): the virgin birth, the refuge upon a peaceful height watered by a living spring. Qushayrī notes that God gathered in this sūra the accounts of the prophets to manifest the unity of their mission; Jesus and Mary are its most delicate sign, where divine grace becomes visible in the very frailty of the creature.

mots.racine: ب ن و / أ م م / أ ي ي
mots.sens: the son of Mary and his mother, a sign
mots.def: ibn Maryam: the son of Mary (Jesus); āya: sign.

mots.racine: ر ب و / ق ر ر / م ع ن
mots.sens: a stable height with a spring

versets.ar: يَا أَيُّهَا الرُّسُلُ كُلُوا مِنَ الطَّيِّبَاتِ وَاعْمَلُوا صَالِحًا إِنِّي بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ
versets.tr: yā ayyuhā-r-rusulu kulū mina-ṭ-ṭayyibāti wa-ʿmalū ṣāliḥan innī bimā taʿmalūna ʿalīmun
versets.trad: O messengers! Eat of the good things and do righteousness. I know well what you do.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī comments: “Eat of the good things” — of what is licit and permitted to you, declared good, on condition that it accord with the dispensation of the Law, of what was licit in their time. Likewise their righteous works: what was in accord with God's command in their age, by their acts, their beliefs, and their states. Pure food and upright act go together: the sanctified body sustains the accepted work, and “I know” recalls that all is done under the gaze.

mots.racine: أ ك ل / ط ي ب
ع م ل / ص ل ح

versets.ar: وَإِنَّ هَٰذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاتَّقُونِ
versets.tr: wa-inna hādhihi ummatukum ummatan wāḥidatan wa-anā rabbukum fa-ttaqūni
versets.trad: And this community of yours is one single community, and I am your Lord, so fear Me.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: your Worshipped is one, your prophet is one, your Law is one; in the foundations you share a single way. Do not take the forks of the roads, lest you fall into the ravines of error. And on “Fear Me”: dread to transgress My command, know the greatness of My power, keep My secret amid the running of the Decree, perpetuate My remembrance in your hearts — you will find at the end My forgiveness, and enjoy My kindness.

mots.racine: أ م م / و ح د
ر ب ب / و ق ي
mots.sens: and I am your Lord, so fear Me

versets.ar: فَتَقَطَّعُوا أَمْرَهُم بَيْنَهُمْ زُبُرًا كُلُّ حِزْبٍ بِمَا لَدَيْهِمْ فَرِحُونَ
versets.tr: fa-taqaṭṭaʿū amrahum baynahum zuburan kullu ḥizbin bimā ladayhim fariḥūna
versets.trad: But they split up their affair among themselves into sects, each faction rejoicing in what it held.
versets.commentaire: Against the willed unity, men shatter their affair into sects. Qushayrī: one is upright in his truth, another lost in his error; each bound to his limit, halted at what was apportioned to him from the origin. Each claims his own way and pretends to hold reality; but when the sky of the hearts of the people of Unity clears, no dust troubles the road, and they are in the certitude of their gnosis, without doubt or ambiguity, while the people of falsehood remain in the blindness of their ignorance and the trial of their doubt.

mots.racine: ق ط ع / ز ب ر
mots.sens: they split their affair into sects
mots.def: taqaṭṭaʿa: to divide; zubur: fragments, sects.

mots.racine: ح ز ب / ف ر ح
mots.sens: each party rejoices in what it has

versets.ar: فَذَرْهُمْ فِي غَمْرَتِهِمْ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ
versets.tr: fa-dharhum fī ghamratihim ḥattā ḥīnin
versets.trad: So leave them in their confusion for a time.
versets.commentaire: “Leave them in their abyss” (ghamra) for a time. Qushayrī warns beneath the appearance of respite: the term of their seizing is near, and the chastisement, when they are taken, will be terrible; soon their error will be set apart for them from their rightness. The “time” granted is no forgetting but a reprieve: the confusion they delight in is already the beginning of their loss.

mots.racine: غ م ر
ح ي ن

versets.ar: أَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّمَا نُمِدُّهُم بِهِ مِن مَّالٍ وَبَنِينَ
versets.tr: a-yaḥsabūna annamā numidduhum bihi min mālin wa-banīna
versets.trad: Do they think that what We supply them with — wealth and sons —
versets.commentaire: The verse opens a question on the illusion of the well-supplied. Qushayrī ties it to the “people of istidrāj” — those whom God leads by degrees to their ruin under the guise of favor: He lays a snare for them in the very road they think safe; they take a mirage for water. Wealth and sons are not, in themselves, signs of approval.

mots.racine: ح س ب
م د د / م و ل / ب ن و
mots.sens: We supply them with wealth and sons
mots.def: amadda: to supply; māl: wealth; banūn: sons.

versets.ar: نُسَارِعُ لَهُمْ فِي الْخَيْرَاتِ بَل لَّا يَشْعُرُونَ
versets.tr: nusāriʿu lahum fī-l-khayrāti bal lā yashʿurūna
versets.trad: is a hastening of good things for them? No, they do not perceive.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī completes his picture of the gradual seduction: this concerns the people of the lure, whom the Real beguiles by disguising the path: they saw a mirage and took it for a draught; a poison was slipped into their honey which they took for sweetness — and when they met the chastisement, they knew they had not acted rightly. Abundance is no favor when it lulls the conscience.

mots.racine: س ر ع / خ ي ر
mots.sens: We hasten good things for them
mots.def: sāraʿa: to hasten; khayrāt: good things.

mots.racine: ش ع ر
mots.def: shaʿara: to perceive, to be aware.

versets.ar: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ هُم مِّنْ خَشْيَةِ رَبِّهِم مُّشْفِقُونَ
versets.tr: inna-lladhīna hum min khashyati rabbihim mushfiqūna
versets.trad: Surely those who, from awe of their Lord, are apprehensive,
versets.commentaire: The mirror-portrait of the true believers begins. Qushayrī: the sign of this apprehension born of awe is the bowing of the secret in the standing before God, with the marks of courtesy and watchfulness against the surprises of rejection; no rest seizes them, so deeply does dread dwell in them, and the reign of reverence (hayba) has taken hold of them. These believers tremble not with servile terror but with the sacred dread of one who stands in the Presence.

mots.racine: خ ش ي / ر ب ب
ش ف ق
mots.def: mushfiq: who fears with tenderness and dread.

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ هُم بِآيَاتِ رَبِّهِمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna hum bi-āyāti rabbihim yuʾminūna
versets.trad: those who believe in the signs of their Lord,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī widens the sense of the « signs »: these signs are diverse: among them are those whose unveiling they receive across the lands, in the variety of the ages and what stirs in men — their aspirations, their wishes, their wills. When the servant believes in them and draws the lesson, he contents himself with what he sees himself answerable for. To believe in the signs is to read the world as a word addressed.

mots.racine: أ ي ي / ر ب ب
mots.sens: in the signs of their Lord

mots.racine: أ م ن
mots.def: āmana: to believe, to give faith.

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ هُم بِرَبِّهِمْ لَا يُشْرِكُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna hum bi-rabbihim lā yushrikūna
versets.trad: those who associate nothing with their Lord,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī hunts down the subtle shirk: they forsake the manifest associationism and the hidden. Hidden shirk is to heed creatures in the act of obedience, to rejoice at their praise, to be cast down when their gaze turns away; it is also to ascribe the rare among states — fortune or misfortune — to secondary causes, as to say: “But for your father's prayer, you would have perished,” or “without so-and-so's effort, I would not have succeeded.” When the servant is certain in his secret that no event proceeds but from the Decree, he comes out of shirk.

mots.racine: ر ب ب
ش ر ك
mots.def: ashraka: to associate, to give God a partner.

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ يُؤْتُونَ مَا آتَوا وَّقُلُوبُهُمْ وَجِلَةٌ أَنَّهُمْ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ رَاجِعُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna yuʾtūna mā ātaw wa-qulūbuhum wajilatun annahum ilā rabbihim rājiʿūna
versets.trad: and those who give what they give while their hearts tremble that to their Lord they are returning:
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī marvels at this paradox of the holy heart: they are sincere in obedience, without the least trace of shortcoming, without halt in the abodes of sloth, without inclination to rest upon dispensations — and yet they fear as though they had committed enormities; they regard their states with an eye of belittling and self-contempt, dreading the surprises of the Decree. And he cites: “He shuns sins, then dreads them — as though his good deeds were sins.”

mots.racine: أ ت ي
ق ل ب / و ج ل
mots.def: wajila: to tremble, to quake with fear.

versets.ar: أُولَٰئِكَ يُسَارِعُونَ فِي الْخَيْرَاتِ وَهُمْ لَهَا سَابِقُونَ
versets.tr: ulāʾika yusāriʿūna fī-l-khayrāti wa-hum lahā sābiqūna
versets.trad: those hasten to good deeds, and they are foremost in them.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī distinguishes three ways of running to the good: he who hastens by his steps, on the side of works; he who hastens by his aspirations, on the side of spiritual unions; he who hastens by his repentance, on the side of swallowed regrets. And all hit the mark: each has, according to his state, his share of the approach. Precedence in the good has several ways, all accepted.

mots.racine: س ر ع / خ ي ر
mots.def: sāraʿa: to race, to vie in speed.

mots.racine: س ب ق

versets.ar: وَلَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا وَلَدَيْنَا كِتَابٌ يَنطِقُ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lā nukallifu nafsan illā wusʿahā wa-ladaynā kitābun yanṭiqu bi-l-ḥaqqi wa-hum lā yuẓlamūna
versets.trad: We charge no soul beyond its capacity. With Us is a Book that speaks the truth, and they shall not be wronged.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī distinguishes the demands of the Law from those of Reality: the obligations of the Law are pledged upon ease; but the demands of Reality, as was said, are nothing less than the gift of the soul. And on the Book that speaks truth: were it not for their distraction far from the places of Reality, He would not have frightened them with the writing of the angels; but they neglected the contemplation of the Real, so He warned them by what the angels inscribe upon them.

mots.racine: ك ل ف / و س ع
mots.sens: We charge a soul only to its capacity
mots.def: kallafa: to impose a burden; wusʿ: capacity.

mots.racine: ك ت ب / ن ط ق / ح ق ق
mots.sens: a Book that speaks the truth

versets.ar: بَلْ قُلُوبُهُمْ فِي غَمْرَةٍ مِّنْ هَٰذَا وَلَهُمْ أَعْمَالٌ مِّن دُونِ ذَٰلِكَ هُمْ لَهَا عَامِلُونَ
versets.tr: bal qulūbuhum fī ghamratin min hādhā wa-lahum aʿmālun min dūni dhālika hum lahā ʿāmilūna
versets.trad: But their hearts are in an abyss concerning this, and they have other deeds, beside that, which they keep doing,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes of this verse a radical demand for availability: none is fit for this affair but one emptied of every occupation, who has no care for this world or the next. He who busies himself with his life here below, or whose heart converses about his Hereafter, has no share in the discourse of his Master. And he cites the hadith: “Two blessings of which many people are deprived: health and leisure.”

mots.racine: غ م ر
ع م ل

versets.ar: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا أَخَذْنَا مُتْرَفِيهِم بِالْعَذَابِ إِذَا هُمْ يَجْأَرُونَ
versets.tr: ḥattā idhā akhadhnā mutrafīhim bi-l-ʿadhābi idhā hum yajʾarūna
versets.trad: until, when We seize their affluent ones with the chastisement, behold, they cry aloud.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sums up God's patient justice: He grants respite but does not neglect; and when He seizes, His grip is violent — “the grip of your Lord is terrible.” When He seizes the guilty, at the hour the vengeance falls, they receive in answer only humiliation. The “cry” (jaʾr) of the affluent is the roar of one who discovers too late the emptiness of his ease.

mots.racine: أ خ ذ / ت ر ف / ع ذ ب
mots.sens: We seized their affluent with the chastisement
mots.def: mutraf: filled with ease; ʿadhāb: chastisement.

mots.racine: ج أ ر
mots.def: jaʾara: to cry out, to bellow.

versets.ar: لَا تَجْأَرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّكُم مِّنَّا لَا تُنصَرُونَ
versets.tr: lā tajʾarū-l-yawma innakum minnā lā tunṣarūna
versets.trad: Do not cry aloud today; you will not be helped against Us.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: when a verdict issues from the Unseen, none can repel His decree. The cry has no grip once the sentence is pronounced. He adds a legal image: to a crime belongs a spreading: if the criminal ceases his act, that avails him nothing so long as the sentence of the damage runs its course. Today, the time of imploring is past.

mots.racine: ج أ ر
mots.def: jaʾara: to cry out, to implore.

mots.racine: ن ص ر
mots.sens: you will not be helped against Us

versets.ar: قَدْ كَانَتْ آيَاتِي تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَكُنتُمْ عَلَىٰ أَعْقَابِكُمْ تَنكِصُونَ
versets.tr: qad kānat āyātī tutlā ʿalaykum fa-kuntum ʿalā aʿqābikum tankiṣūna
versets.trad: My signs were recited to you, but you used to turn back on your heels,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: this belongs to the exhausting of the excuse and the establishing of the proof: it is settled that henceforth neither lamentation will avail, nor will excuse be heard. Kings, when they have sealed a judgment, are unmoved in what issues from them by any imploring. To turn one's heels (nukūṣ) on the recited signs is knowingly to regress, far from the light offered.

mots.racine: أ ي ي / ت ل و
mots.sens: My signs were recited to you
mots.def: talā: to recite; āyāt: verses, signs.

mots.racine: ع ق ب / ن ك ص
mots.sens: you turned back on your heels
mots.def: nakaṣa: to turn back, to regress; aʿqāb: heels.

versets.ar: مُسْتَكْبِرِينَ بِهِ سَامِرًا تَهْجُرُونَ
versets.tr: mustakbirīna bihi sāmiran tahjurūna
versets.trad: swollen with pride toward it, talking nonsense by night, reviling it.
versets.commentaire: Arrogance (istikbār) and nights spent reviling the Word. Qushayrī inscribes this attitude in the tableau of obstinacy: having made arrogance their dwelling, they turn the night — the time of intimacy with God for the believers — into a time of slander against His signs. The turning-away becomes habit, and habit a second nature.

mots.racine: ك ب ر
س م ر / ه ج ر
mots.def: sāmir: who stays up talking; hajara: to utter abusive talk.

versets.ar: أَفَلَمْ يَدَّبَّرُوا الْقَوْلَ أَمْ جَاءَهُم مَّا لَمْ يَأْتِ آبَاءَهُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ
versets.tr: a-fa-lam yaddabbarū-l-qawla am jāʾahum mā lam yaʾti ābāʾahumu-l-awwalīna
versets.trad: Have they not pondered the Word, or has there come to them what came not to their forefathers of old?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: had they looked well, had they brought upon their states a sound thought, they would have seen clearly at once, and the opacity and the riddle would have lifted from their hearts. But they took up residence on the mount of sloth, halted at the abodes of heedlessness: they grew accustomed to ignorance and despaired of clear sight. To ponder the Word (tadabbur al-qawl) would have sufficed to awaken them.

mots.racine: د ب ر / ق و ل
mots.sens: have they not pondered the Word?
mots.def: tadabbara: to ponder, to consider; qawl: word.

mots.racine: أ ت ي / أ ب و
mots.sens: what came not to their first fathers

versets.ar: أَمْ لَمْ يَعْرِفُوا رَسُولَهُمْ فَهُمْ لَهُ مُنكِرُونَ
versets.tr: am lam yaʿrifū rasūlahum fa-hum lahu munkirūna
versets.trad: Or did they not recognize their Messenger, that they should deny him?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they turned from reality and strayed into the valleys of error; false conjectures led them astray, lying suppositions possessed them. At times they met him with denial, at times charged him with magic, at times reproached him for eating and drinking as men do, at times disparaged him for his poverty. Their denial of the Messenger they yet know betrays the scattering of their hearts.

mots.racine: ع ر ف / ر س ل
mots.sens: they did not recognize their messenger

mots.racine: ن ك ر

versets.ar: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ بِهِ جِنَّةٌ بَلْ جَاءَهُم بِالْحَقِّ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كَارِهُونَ
versets.tr: am yaqūlūna bihi jinnatun bal jāʾahum bi-l-ḥaqqi wa-aktharuhum li-l-ḥaqqi kārihūna
versets.trad: Or do they say he is possessed? No, he has come to them with the Truth, but most of them are averse to the Truth.
versets.commentaire: The verse dismantles the charge of madness: he has come “with the Truth” (bi-l-ḥaqq), and it is the Truth itself they detest. In Qushayrī's perspective, the refusal is no lack of evidence but an aversion of the heart: the sick soul hates the light that judges it. It is not the messenger they reject, but the truth he bears, which troubles them.

mots.racine: ق و ل / ج ن ن
mots.sens: do they say he is possessed?

mots.racine: ك ر ه / ح ق ق
mots.sens: most of them detest the Truth
mots.def: kārih: who is averse; ḥaqq: Truth.

versets.ar: وَلَوِ اتَّبَعَ الْحَقُّ أَهْوَاءَهُمْ لَفَسَدَتِ السَّمَاوَاتُ وَالْأَرْضُ وَمَن فِيهِنَّ بَلْ أَتَيْنَاهُم بِذِكْرِهِمْ فَهُمْ عَن ذِكْرِهِم مُّعْرِضُونَ
versets.tr: wa-law-ittabaʿa-l-ḥaqqu ahwāʾahum la-fasadati-s-samāwātu wa-l-arḍu wa-man fīhinna bal ataynāhum bi-dhikrihim fa-hum ʿan dhikrihim muʿriḍūna
versets.trad: And had the Truth followed their desires, the heavens and the earth and all within them would have fallen into ruin. Nay, We brought them their Reminder, but from their Reminder they turn away.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: this is owing to the contradiction of their desires and passions: they contradict one another in their demands and aims, and their simultaneous fulfilment is impossible. God has shown that, were He to make His decree reign according to their whims, the order of the heavens and the earth would break, departing from all soundness and harmony. The Truth does not bend to passion, for the whole universe rests upon the order it institutes.

mots.racine: ت ب ع / ه و ي
ف س د
mots.sens: heavens and earth would be ruined
mots.def: fasada: to be corrupted, to fall into disorder.

versets.ar: أَمْ تَسْأَلُهُمْ خَرْجًا فَخَرَاجُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ
versets.tr: am tasʾaluhum kharjan fa-kharāju rabbika khayrun wa-huwa khayru-r-rāziqīna
versets.trad: Or do you ask of them a payment? But your Lord's reward is better, and He is the best of providers.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: you ask of them no wage for conveying the message; this was the way of the prophets: they worked for God and asked no wage but of God. And the scholars are the heirs of the prophets: their road is to guard against the defilement of covetousness and against eating by means of religion — for that is an ostentation harmful to faith. When the work is for God, the wage is awaited from God. Here Qushayrī rebukes the greedy preachers of his time.

mots.racine: س أ ل / خ ر ج
mots.sens: you ask of them a payment

mots.racine: خ ي ر / ر ز ق

versets.ar: وَإِنَّكَ لَتَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ
versets.tr: wa-innaka la-tadʿūhum ilā ṣirāṭin mustaqīmin
versets.trad: And surely you call them to a straight path,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī defines the « straight path » at its highest: the contemplation of the Lord in His absolute oneness, in all things and in the act of bringing into being; and submission to the verdicts of the Decree by the heart's assent, without felt constraint. The ṣirāṭ mustaqīm is not only moral rectitude, but the total orientation of being toward the One, where the servant's will weds that of God.

mots.racine: د ع و
ص ر ط / ق و م

versets.ar: وَإِنَّ الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ عَنِ الصِّرَاطِ لَنَاكِبُونَ
versets.tr: wa-inna-lladhīna lā yuʾminūna bi-l-ākhirati ʿani-ṣ-ṣirāṭi la-nākibūna
versets.trad: but those who do not believe in the Hereafter deviate from the path.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they have deviated from the supreme argument by their hearts, and have fallen into the hell of division; tomorrow their feet will slip from the Bridge and skid, and they will plunge into the fire of burning. They are deviants (nākibūn) in their here below as in their Hereafter. The denial of the last life is no neutral opinion: it makes the whole path swerve.

mots.racine: أ م ن / أ خ ر
mots.sens: they do not believe in the Hereafter

mots.racine: ن ك ب

versets.ar: وَلَوْ رَحِمْنَاهُمْ وَكَشَفْنَا مَا بِهِم مِّن ضُرٍّ لَّلَجُّوا فِي طُغْيَانِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ
versets.tr: wa-law raḥimnāhum wa-kashafnā mā bihim min ḍurrin la-lajjū fī ṭughyānihim yaʿmahūna
versets.trad: And had We shown them mercy and lifted the harm afflicting them, they would persist, blindly wandering in their rebellion.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He informs of His true knowledge of them, knowledge issuing from His prior decree concerning them: “were We to dispel their harm now, they would not hold to the faith they promise themselves.” He knew they would disbelieve, and decreed that they would disbelieve — for it is inconceivable that His decree concerning them contradict His knowledge of them. Even deliverance would not reform a heart resolved on wandering.

mots.racine: ر ح م
ل ج ج / ط غ ي
mots.sens: they would persist in their excess
mots.def: lajja: to persist; ṭughyān: rebellion, excess.

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْنَاهُم بِالْعَذَابِ فَمَا اسْتَكَانُوا لِرَبِّهِمْ وَمَا يَتَضَرَّعُونَ
versets.tr: wa-laqad akhadhnāhum bi-l-ʿadhābi fa-mā-stakānū li-rabbihim wa-mā yataḍarraʿūna
versets.trad: We seized them with the chastisement, yet they did not humble themselves to their Lord, nor did they entreat.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We made them taste the first-fruits of the chastisement, not its rigors, to awaken them — but they neither awoke nor took heed. Had they, on seeing the chastisement, had recourse to supplication and entreaty, God would have hastened its lifting; but they persisted in their falsehood, so that God might accomplish a decree already settled. The trial willed humility; they answered with hardening.

mots.racine: أ خ ذ / ع ذ ب
mots.sens: We seized them with the chastisement

mots.racine: س ك ن
mots.def: istakāna: to make oneself humble, to submit.

versets.ar: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَابًا ذَا عَذَابٍ شَدِيدٍ إِذَا هُمْ فِيهِ مُبْلِسُونَ
versets.tr: ḥattā idhā fataḥnā ʿalayhim bāban dhā ʿadhābin shadīdin idhā hum fīhi mublisūna
versets.trad: until, when We open upon them a gate of severe chastisement, behold, they are plunged in despair.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: when We brought down upon them the harshest chastisements, they weakened to bear them; they were seized unawares, and what they had advanced of imploring availed no more; they despaired of being answered and halted at the abodes of dejection (qunūṭ). The “gate” opened upon severe chastisement is the one beyond which all hope is extinguished.

mots.ar: فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَابًا ذَا عَذَابٍ شَدِيدٍ
mots.tr: fataḥnā ʿalayhim bāban dhā ʿadhābin shadīd
mots.racine: ف ت ح / ع ذ ب / ش د د
mots.sens: We opened on them a gate of severe chastisement

mots.racine: ب ل س

versets.ar: وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنشَأَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصَارَ وَالْأَفْئِدَةَ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-huwa-lladhī anshaʾa lakumu-s-samʿa wa-l-abṣāra wa-l-afʾidata qalīlan mā tashkurūna
versets.trad: It is He who brought into being for you hearing and sight and hearts. Little do you give thanks!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes of each faculty a place of active gratitude: their thanks for them is to employ them in His obedience. The thanks of hearing is to hear only by God and for God; the thanks of sight, to look only by God for God; the thanks of the heart, to contemplate only God and to love by it only God. The “little thanks” reproaches the profane use of organs made for the sacred.

mots.racine: س م ع / ب ص ر / ف أ د
mots.def: samʿ: hearing; abṣār: sights; afʾida: inmost hearts.

mots.racine: ق ل ل / ش ك ر

versets.ar: وَهُوَ الَّذِي ذَرَأَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-huwa-lladhī dharaʾakum fī-l-arḍi wa-ilayhi tuḥsharūna
versets.trad: It is He who scattered you upon the earth, and to Him you shall be gathered.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī condenses here the whole doctrine of Unity: the beginning of beings is from God by creation, and their end returns to Him; tawḥīd orders these senses: know that phenomena are by God as to manifestation, to God as to ownership, from God as to beginning, and to God as to end. Scattered then gathered: man is wholly suspended between a divine origin and a divine return.

mots.racine: ذ ر أ / أ ر ض
mots.sens: He scattered you on the earth
mots.def: dharaʾa: to disseminate, to create in multitude.

mots.racine: ح ش ر
mots.sens: to Him you shall be gathered

versets.ar: وَهُوَ الَّذِي يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ وَلَهُ اخْتِلَافُ اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهَارِ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ
versets.tr: wa-huwa-lladhī yuḥyī wa-yumītu wa-lahu-khtilāfu-l-layli wa-n-nahāri a-fa-lā taʿqilūna
versets.trad: It is He who gives life and gives death, and to Him belongs the alternation of night and day. Will you not reason?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī transposes life and death to the plane of the heart: He gives life to souls and gives them death — and likewise the hearts: the death of the heart is unbelief and denial; its life is faith and Unity. And as the hearts have their life and death, so the moments have their death and life: the life of moments by the blessing of His turning toward us, their death by the trial of His turning away. And he cites: “I die when I recall You, then I revive — how often I revive for You, how often I die!”

mots.racine: ح ي ي / م و ت
mots.sens: He gives life and gives death
mots.def: aḥyā: to give life; amāta: to give death.

mots.racine: خ ل ف / ل ي ل / ن ه ر
mots.sens: the alternation of night and day

versets.ar: بَلْ قَالُوا مِثْلَ مَا قَالَ الْأَوَّلُونَ
versets.tr: bal qālū mithla mā qāla-l-awwalūna
versets.trad: Nay, they say the like of what the ancients said,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they took, in denial, the way of their predecessors, and exceeded in obstinacy as those exceeded — so there reached them what reached the first: their annihilation and their ruin. To take up the speech of the ancients in denial is to inherit their fate by inheriting their error.

mots.racine: ق و ل
م ث ل / أ و ل

versets.ar: قَالُوا أَإِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا وَعِظَامًا أَإِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ
versets.tr: qālū a-idhā mitnā wa-kunnā turāban wa-ʿiẓāman a-innā la-mabʿūthūna
versets.trad: They say: “When we are dead and become dust and bones, shall we indeed be raised?
versets.commentaire: The ritual objection returns word for word. Qushayrī treats it as the symptom of an intelligence riveted to the sensible, unable to conceive that the recommencing is as possible as the commencing. Dust and bones are no obstacle to God; they are so only for one who measures infinite Power by the gauge of his own weakness.

mots.racine: م و ت / ت ر ب / ع ظ م
ب ع ث

versets.ar: لَقَدْ وُعِدْنَا نَحْنُ وَآبَاؤُنَا هَٰذَا مِن قَبْلُ إِنْ هَٰذَا إِلَّا أَسَاطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ
versets.tr: laqad wuʿidnā naḥnu wa-ābāʾunā hādhā min qablu in hādhā illā asāṭīru-l-awwalīna
versets.trad: We and our fathers were promised this before; these are but tales of the ancients!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the remoteness of the time of Gathering and of the chastisement promised after resurrection only increased their doubt; they made it an argument for their confusion and their turmoil, saying: “We were promised this, we and our fathers, and nothing came to pass; we are but their like.” The very delay of fulfilment serves them as a pretext for denial.

mots.racine: و ع د / أ ب و
mots.sens: we and our fathers were promised

mots.racine: س ط ر / أ و ل

versets.ar: قُل لِّمَنِ الْأَرْضُ وَمَن فِيهَا إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ
versets.tr: qul li-mani-l-arḍu wa-man fīhā in kuntum taʿlamūna
versets.trad: Say: “To whom belong the earth and all who are in it, if you know?”
versets.commentaire: God commands the Prophet, Qushayrī says, to vary the questions upon them so as to corner them into their own admission. The first interrogates ownership: to whom the earth and its dwellers? The argument proceeds from what they already admit, to lead them toward what they deny. The creation acknowledged proves the resurrection refused.

mots.racine: أ ر ض
mots.sens: to whom the earth and those in it?

mots.racine: ع ل م

versets.ar: سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ قُلْ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ
versets.tr: sa-yaqūlūna lillāhi qul a-fa-lā tadhakkarūna
versets.trad: They will say: “To God.” Say: “Will you not then remember?”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī notes the order of the reproaches: He first said “will you not remember?” then “will you not fear?”, placing remembrance (tadhakkur) before fear, for it is by remembrance that they reach forgiveness; and after they have known Him, it falls to them to fear disobeying Him. Their very admission — “to God” — becomes the accuser of their forgetting.

mots.racine: ق و ل
ذ ك ر

versets.ar: قُلْ مَن رَّبُّ السَّمَاوَاتِ السَّبْعِ وَرَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ
versets.tr: qul man rabbu-s-samāwāti-s-sabʿi wa-rabbu-l-ʿarshi-l-ʿaẓīmi
versets.trad: Say: “Who is the Lord of the seven heavens and the Lord of the mighty Throne?”
versets.commentaire: The second question rises higher: who is the Lord of the seven heavens and of the mighty Throne? Qushayrī shows the gradation: from earth to Throne, from the visible to the highest. The interrogation encloses the adversary in his own acknowledgment, for none can ascribe the lordship of the cosmos to other than God — and that lordship acknowledged ruins all idolatry in advance.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / س م و / س ب ع
ع ر ش / ع ظ م

versets.ar: سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ قُلْ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ
versets.tr: sa-yaqūlūna lillāhi qul a-fa-lā tattaqūna
versets.trad: They will say: “To God.” Say: “Will you not then fear?”
versets.commentaire: After the admission of lordship, the reproach sharpens: “will you not fear?” Qushayrī: having known God through remembrance, they must now fear Him, for knowledge without fear is barren. To acknowledge the Lord of the Throne and persist in offending Him is the very incoherence the verse lays bare.

mots.racine: ق و ل
و ق ي

versets.ar: قُلْ مَن بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَهُوَ يُجِيرُ وَلَا يُجَارُ عَلَيْهِ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ
versets.tr: qul man bi-yadihi malakūtu kulli shayʾin wa-huwa yujīru wa-lā yujāru ʿalayhi in kuntum taʿlamūna
versets.trad: Say: “Who holds in His hand the dominion of all things, granting protection while none can protect against Him, if you know?”
versets.commentaire: The third question touches the ultimate sovereignty: who holds the malakūt — the hidden kingdom of all things — and grants asylum while none grants it to Him? Qushayrī sees in this series a pedagogy of forced admission: at each interrogation, they can answer only “God,” and each answer tightens the noose of their contradiction.

mots.racine: ي د ي / م ل ك
mots.sens: in His hand the dominion of all things

mots.racine: ج و ر
mots.sens: He protects and none protects against Him
mots.def: ajāra: to grant asylum, to protect.

versets.ar: سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ قُلْ فَأَنَّىٰ تُسْحَرُونَ
versets.tr: sa-yaqūlūna lillāhi qul fa-annā tusḥarūna
versets.trad: They will say: “To God.” Say: “How then are you bewitched?”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: after the clearness of the proof, what doubt remains for you to ascribe it to magic? “How are you bewitched?” — that is, after the light has risen, what spell turns you away? The irony is cutting: they who accused the Prophet of sorcery are sent back to their own bewitchment — the blindness of one who acknowledges the truth and refuses it.

mots.racine: ق و ل
س ح ر

versets.ar: بَلْ أَتَيْنَاهُم بِالْحَقِّ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكَاذِبُونَ
versets.tr: bal ataynāhum bi-l-ḥaqqi wa-innahum la-kādhibūna
versets.trad: Nay, We have brought them the Truth, but they are surely liars.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He has shown that they persisted in their denial, kept up their insolence and rebellion; and after the excuses have been removed, it is no longer time for excuse, and nothing justifies the least leniency. The Truth (ḥaqq) has been brought to them unequivocally; their lie is therefore no longer error, but deliberate refusal.

mots.racine: أ ت ي / ح ق ق
mots.def: atā bi: to bring; ḥaqq: truth.

mots.racine: ك ذ ب

versets.ar: مَا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ مِن وَلَدٍ وَمَا كَانَ مَعَهُ مِنْ إِلَٰهٍ إِذًا لَّذَهَبَ كُلُّ إِلَٰهٍ بِمَا خَلَقَ وَلَعَلَا بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ
versets.tr: mā-ttakhadha-llāhu min waladin wa-mā kāna maʿahu min ilāhin idhan la-dhahaba kullu ilāhin bimā khalaqa wa-la-ʿalā baʿḍuhum ʿalā baʿḍin subḥāna-llāhi ʿammā yaṣifūna
versets.trad: God has taken no son, nor is there any god with Him; otherwise each god would have taken away what it created, and some would have risen over others. Glory be to God above what they describe!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: to take a son is as impossible as to take a partner; both fall within the impossible, for son or partner would imply equality in power, and the Ṣamadiyya (the divine Absoluteness) is sanctified above any like or any genus. And he adds: every affair hung upon two loses its order and its right hierarchy — the argument of mutual hindrance refutes the plurality of gods.

mots.racine: أ خ ذ / و ل د
mots.def: ittakhadha: to take, to adopt; walad: child.

mots.racine: س ب ح / و ص ف
mots.sens: glory be to God above their descriptions
mots.def: subḥān: transcendence, glory; waṣafa: to describe.

versets.ar: عَالِمِ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهَادَةِ فَتَعَالَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ
versets.tr: ʿālimi-l-ghaybi wa-sh-shahādati fa-taʿālā ʿammā yushrikūna
versets.trad: Knower of the unseen and the witnessed; exalted is He above what they associate.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “Knower of the unseen and the witnessed”: He transcends the imaginings of the one who associates and the conjectures of the one who lies. The knowledge that embraces at once the hidden (ghayb) and the manifest (shahāda) is the seal of Oneness: only He who knows all, without gap, deserves exclusive worship. Transcendence (taʿālī) completes the refutation of associationism.

mots.racine: ع ل م / غ ي ب / ش ه د
mots.sens: Knower of the unseen and the witnessed

mots.racine: ع ل و / ش ر ك

versets.ar: قُل رَّبِّ إِمَّا تُرِيَنِّي مَا يُوعَدُونَ
versets.tr: qul rabbi immā turiyannī mā yūʿadūna
versets.trad: Say: “My Lord! If You would show me what they are promised...
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: he says: “If You hasten upon them what You threaten them with, do not place me among them, do not bring upon me an evil like the punishment You bring upon them.” In this is a proof that the Real may do what He wills: were He to chastise the innocent, that would be from Him neither injustice nor ugliness. The Prophet's prayer manifests reverent fear before the absolute freedom of God.

mots.racine: ر أ ي
mots.def: arā: to show, to make see.

mots.racine: و ع د

versets.ar: رَبِّ فَلَا تَجْعَلْنِي فِي الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ
versets.tr: rabbi fa-lā tajʿalnī fī-l-qawmi-ẓ-ẓālimīna
versets.trad: my Lord, do not place me among the wrongdoing people!”
versets.commentaire: The prayer continues: not to be counted among the unjust at the moment of chastisement. In Qushayrī's spirit, this is the avowal that none, even a prophet, has a guarantee upon God: preservation is pure grace, not an acquired right. To ask not to be “among the wrongdoers” is the mark of the servant who never rests upon his works.

mots.racine: ج ع ل
ظ ل م

versets.ar: وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰ أَن نُّرِيَكَ مَا نَعِدُهُمْ لَقَادِرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-innā ʿalā an nuriyaka mā naʿiduhum la-qādirūna
versets.trad: And surely We are able to show you what We promise them.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī draws from this verse a theological subtlety: it proves the validity of His Power over the contrary of what He knows; for He announced that He could hasten their chastisement, then did not do so — thus Power is proven over the contrary of the known. God can do what He has resolved not to do: His knowledge does not chain His omnipotence.

mots.racine: ر أ ي / و ع د
mots.sens: to show you what We promise them
mots.def: arā: to show; waʿada: to promise.

mots.racine: ق د ر

versets.ar: ادْفَعْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ السَّيِّئَةَ نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَصِفُونَ
versets.tr: idfaʿ bi-llatī hiya aḥsanu-s-sayyiʾata naḥnu aʿlamu bimā yaṣifūna
versets.trad: Repel evil with that which is better. We know best what they describe.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unfolds several senses: repel the offense with fidelity, the crime of the rebels with the law of beneficence; take the road of generosity, do not incline toward the way of requital. And he rises inward: the “better” is what the heart inspires, the “evil” what the soul summons; the better is what Reality guides, the evil what the demon whispers; the better is the light of realities, the evil the darkness of creatures.

mots.racine: د ف ع / ح س ن / س و أ
mots.sens: repel evil with what is better
mots.def: dafaʿa: to repel; sayyiʾa: evil deed.

mots.racine: ع ل م / و ص ف
mots.sens: We know best what they utter

versets.ar: وَقُل رَّبِّ أَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنْ هَمَزَاتِ الشَّيَاطِينِ
versets.tr: wa-qul rabbi aʿūdhu bika min hamazāti-sh-shayāṭīni
versets.trad: And say: “My Lord! I seek refuge in You from the promptings of the devils,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī probes the nature of refuge: true istiʿādha is by God against God, as the Prophet said: “I seek refuge in You from You.” But He willed that we worship Him by seeking refuge in Him from the devil, and from all that is set over us. And he adds: had the devil any power to lead astray, he would begin by guarding himself from perdition — whoever is unable to save himself is yet more unable to lead another astray.

mots.racine: ع و ذ
ه م ز / ش ط ن

versets.ar: وَأَعُوذُ بِكَ رَبِّ أَن يَحْضُرُونِ
versets.tr: wa-aʿūdhu bika rabbi an yaḥḍurūni
versets.trad: and I seek refuge in You, my Lord, lest they be present with me.”
versets.commentaire: The refuge grows precise: that they be not even present. In Qushayrī's reading, it is not only the suggestion one dreads, but the very intrusive presence that troubles intimacy with God. The servant asks not to be protected after the assault, but that the assault not occur — that nothing come between the heart and its Lord.

mots.racine: ح ض ر
mots.sens: lest they be present with me

mots.racine: ر ب ب

versets.ar: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا جَاءَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قَالَ رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ
versets.tr: ḥattā idhā jāʾa aḥadahumu-l-mawtu qāla rabbi-rjiʿūni
versets.trad: until, when death comes to one of them, he says: “My Lord, send me back,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: when the trial takes them by the throat, when harm seizes their states and they know there is no escape nor refuge, they fall to entreaty and abasement — but what they seek is more inaccessible than tearing thorns with bare hands. And he cites: “I said to the soul: if you would return, return before the road is closed.”

mots.racine: ج ي أ / م و ت
mots.sens: death came to one of them

mots.racine: ر ج ع
mots.def: rajaʿa: to return; arjiʿūni: send me back.

versets.ar: لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صَالِحًا فِيمَا تَرَكْتُ كَلَّا إِنَّهَا كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قَائِلُهَا وَمِن وَرَائِهِم بَرْزَخٌ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ
versets.tr: laʿallī aʿmalu ṣāliḥan fīmā taraktu kallā innahā kalimatun huwa qāʾiluhā wa-min warāʾihim barzakhun ilā yawmi yubʿathūna
